As per this,Warren Hastings was appointed as the Governor-General of the Presidency of Fort William (Bengal).

questions.question: When was the Defence of India Act enacted?
questions.explanation: 1915. The Defence of India Act (1915) - It was an emergency law that gave the British government in India wide powers to suppress dissent and maintain public order during World War I. It was passed by the then Governor General of India Lord Hardinge.

questions.question: Which Act declared that British rule in India would end by 30 June 1948?
questions.explanation: Act of 1947. The Indian Independence Act 1947 was an Act of Parliament of the United Kingdom that granted independence to British India. The Act was passed on July 18, 1947, and came into effect on August 15, 1947. The Act divided British India into two independent dominions, India and Pakistan.

questions.question: In India which Act divided the powers between the Central and units in terms of three lists?
questions.explanation: 1935. Go v ernment of India Act, 1935 : Contain three lists, Federal List (for Centre containing 59 items), Provincial List (for provinces containing 54 items) and the Concurrent List (for both containing 36 items). Q.602 . In which of the following years did the East India Company abolished the diarchy system of Bengal? SSC CHSL 11/08/2023 (1st shift) (a) 1772 (b) 1765 (c) 1757 (d) 1779 Sol.602.(a) 1772 . The system of Dual Government: Introduced in Bengal by Robert Clive of British East India Company. It lasted from 1765 to 1772. Under this system, the administration of Bengal was divided into Nizamat and Diwani. The Diwani was carried out by the company and the Nizamat by the Nizam.

questions.question: Ryotwari system of revenue collection in India, introduced by the British, was based on the_______.
questions.explanation: Ricar dian theor y of r ent - The land of higher fertility gets higher rent than less fertile land. Ry otwari System - The peasants or cultivators were regarded as the owners of the land. The cultivators had to pay annual taxes directly to the government. Introduced by Thomas Munro and Alexander Read in 1820 in the Madras and Bombay presidency.

questions.question: When was the Secretary of State for India made responsible for the Government of British India by bringing about changes in the Home Government?
questions.explanation: 1858. Char ter Act 1858 - The term Governor General changed to Viceroy for the British crown. The ﬁrst vicer oy was Lord Canning. The act ended ‘The Doctrine of Lapse’. It ended the Dual government scheme initiated due to Pitt’s India Act.

questions.question: In 1942, the British government sent the___________mission to India.
questions.explanation: Cripps. It was sent to India to secure Indian cooperation for the British war efforts. It was headed by Sir Richard Stafford Cripps. Gandhi called it 'A post-dated cheque drawn on a failing bank'.

questions.question: The result of the English Education Act of 1835 was to make ________ as a medium of instruction for ___________ education.
questions.explanation: English, Higher . English Education Act (1835) was proposed by William Bentinck on the Advice of Macaulay. Reforms b y William Bentick : The abolition of Sati, the suppression of female infanticide and Thuggee, the abolition of lawlessness, and the abolition of human sacri ﬁ ces. Q.607 . The Charter Act of 1813 extended the East India Company's rule in India to another _________ years. SSC MTS 11/05/2023 (Afternoon) (a) 15 (b) 5 (c) 20 (d) 10 Sol.607.(c) 20. The Char ter Act of 1813 (The East Indian Company Act 1813) - F eatur es: It ended the monopoly of the East India Company over commercial trade except trade with China and trade in tea with India.

questions.question: The objective of Simon Commission was to________.
questions.explanation: Simon Commission - It was appointed by the British Government in 1927 under the chairmanship of John Simon (other members were Pathick Lawrence and Clement Atlee) to review the workings of Government of India (GOI) Act, 1919 and suggest measures.

questions.question: Which act of the British Parliament was also known as East India Company Act 1793?
questions.explanation: The Char ter Act, 1793. The act was passed by the British Parliament to renew the charter of the East India Company to trade within India for next 20 years. It was passed during the tenure of Sir John Shore (1793 -1798).

questions.question: In __________, the British government enacted a legislation banning the use of printed cotton textiles - chintz - in England. Interestingly, this Act was known as the Calico Act.
questions.explanation: 1720. The cotton textiles which the Portuguese took back to Europe, along with the spices, came to be called "calico". Calico Act , Reason - Textile makers in England were Unable to compete with Indian textiles. Highest cotton producing state (Gujarat).

questions.question: In which year was a law passed in India to make conversion to Christianity easier?
questions.explanation: 1850. This law allowed Indians who had converted to Christianity to inherit the property of hisancestors. The Char ter Act of 1813 allowed Chirstian missionaries to enter India and Preach Christianity. This Act asserted the Crown's sovereignty over British possessions in India.

questions.question: Which of the following Indian states was the ﬁrst to sign a subsidiary alliance with the British?
questions.explanation: Hy der abad. In this system, an Indian ruler had to maintain British troops in his state, either by giving some of his territories or by paying for the maintenance of the troops. It was ﬁrst intr oduced by the French East India Company Governor Joseph Francois Dupleix, Later used by Lord Wellesley, Governor-General of India (1798 to 1805).

questions.question: What is the original name of the Empress of India Act and when was it instituted?
questions.explanation: The Ro y al Titles Act 1876 was an Act of the Parliament of the United Kingdom which o ﬃ cially recognized Queen Victoria (and subsequent monarchs) as “Empr ess of India ”. This act was introduced by Benjamin Disraeli. It was repealed on 22 June 1948.

questions.question: Which of the following Acts of 1947 regulates the Indian labor law with regard to trade unions as well as individual workmen?
questions.explanation: Industrial disputes Act 1947. The Rowlatt Act 1919 authorized the arrest, for 2 years without trial, of any person suspected of terrorism living in British India. Indian Council acts (1861, 1892, and 1909). Charter Acts (1793, 1813, 1833, and 1853).

questions.question: Which Act passed by British Government of India abolished the Council of India?
questions.explanation: Go v ernment of India Act 1935 were as follows: Abolition of provincial dyarchy and introduction of dyarchy at centre. Abolition of Indian Council and introduction of an advisory body in its place. Provision for an All India Federation with British India territories and princely states.

questions.question: Which Act was passed in 1929 prohibiting marriage of girls below 14 and boys below 18 years of age?
questions.explanation: Shar da act . The act ﬁx ed the marriageable age for girls at 14 years and 18 years for boys. It was sponsor by Harbilas Sarda. Pitt’s India act (1784), Rowlatt Act (1919), Vernacular press act (1878).

questions.question: In which year through the Indian Oﬃ cial Secrets Act was freedom of press curbed?
questions.explanation: 1904. It was enacted during the time of Lord Curzon, Viceroy of India from 1899 to 1905 curbed the freedom of Press. Later this act was replaced by the O ﬃ cial Secrets act 1923.

questions.question: In 1877, which of the following viceroys organized a durbar to acknowledge Queen Victoria as the Empress of India?
questions.explanation: Lor d L ytt on organized a durbar to acknowledge Queen Victoria as the Empress of India. This was the culmination of the transfer of control of British India from the East India Company to the Crown.

questions.question: The capital of the British Indian Empire was transferred from Calcutta to Delhi in:
questions.explanation: 1911 . The British government believed that ruling India from Delhi was easier and more convenient than from Calcutta. Therefore, Delhi was o ﬃ cially announced as the capital of British Raj by Emperor George V, on December 12, 1911.

questions.question: By which British statute were Indian representatives, for the ﬁrst time, allocated seats for election in Indian Legislative Councils?
questions.explanation: Indian Councils Act, 1892. The act made a limited and indirect provision for the use of election in ﬁlling up some of the nono ﬃ cial seats both in the Central and provincial legislative councils.

questions.question: Which law was imposed in India during the command of General Dyer?
questions.explanation: Mar tial law. General Dyer enacted an Act on April 13, 1919, called martial law stating that no more than 2 people can form a group and meet at a place.

questions.question: In which year was the o ﬃ ce of the ﬁrst Accountant General established in India?
questions.explanation: In 1858 (the year the British took over administrative control of India from the East India Company) the o ﬃ ce of the ﬁrst Accountant General was established in India.

topic: Partition of Bengal and Swadeshi Movements

questions.question: In 1905, Hindus and Muslims tied ‘Rakhi’ on each other’s hands to show their unity and their protest against the _________.
questions.explanation: P ar tition of Bengal. Rabindranath Tagore encouraged Hindus and Muslims to tie rakhis as a symbol of unity and resistance against the Partition of Bengal, promoting a beautiful message of harmony and togetherness. Rowlatt Act 1919 was passed to curb the growing nationalist upsurge in British India. The Morley-Minto Reforms, alsoknown as the Indian Councils Act of 1909, were a set of constitutional changes in British India that aimed to increase Indian participation in the legislature.

questions.question: What was the Swadeshi Movement called in the state of Andhra Pradesh?
questions.explanation: V ande Matar am Mo v ement. The Swadeshi Movement started in August 1905 from Calcutta Town Hall against Lord Curzon’s decision of dividing the province of Bengal. The partition led to protest meetings in Bengal under which the pledge to boycott foreign goods was ﬁrst taken. Leaders of the movement - Poona and Bombay (Lokmanya Tilak), Punjab (Lala Lajpat Rai and Ajit Singh), Delhi (Syed Haider Raza), Madras (Chidambaram Pillai).

questions.question: What is the o ﬃ cial reason given by Britishers after partition of Bengal in 1905?
questions.explanation: Administr ativ e Conv enience. The real reason for the partition was to divide and weaken the growing nationalist movement in Bengal. After Partition Swadeshi movement gained even more momentum, and the British were forced to revoke the partition in 1911.

questions.question: Before the Partition of Bengal in 1905, Bengal was the biggest province of British India. It consisted of parts of which of the following states?
questions.explanation: Bihar and Orissa. Partition of Bengal in 1905 {Lord Curzon announced the partition of Bengal on 20th July 1905, reorganization separated the largely Muslim eastern areas from the largely Hindu western areas}.

questions.question: At which of the following places was the formal proclamation of the Swadeshi movement made on 7 August 1905 ?
questions.explanation: Calcutta T own Hall. The Swadeshi movement was a direct fallout of the decision of the British India government to partition Bengal. The features of the Swadeshi movement : encourage the use of indigenous goods, boycott foreign goods. The partition of Bengal was annulled in 1911 by Lor d Har dinge to curb the revolutionaries.

questions.question: Which of the following parties supported the move for partition of Bengal?
questions.explanation: All India Muslim League supported the move for partition of Bengal. Founders of All India Muslim League are Muhammad Ali Jinnah, Aga Khan III, Khwaja Salimullah, Hakim Ajmal Khan, Waqar-ul-Mulk. It was founded on 30 December 1906.

questions.question: Moved by the Swadeshi Movement, ______ painted his famous image of Bharat Mata portraying her as an ascetic ﬁgur e.
questions.explanation: Abanindr anath T agor e painted his famous image of Bharat Mata portraying her as an ascetic ﬁgur e in 1905. The painting is depicted as a saffron-clad divine woman, holding a book, sheaves of paddy, a piece of white cloth, and a rosary in her four hands.

questions.question: Which of the following places is related to Gandhi’s Satyagraha of the year 1917?
questions.explanation: Champar an. India's ﬁrst Civil Disobedience movement, launched by Mahatma Gandhi in 1917, was prompted by Pandit Raj Kumar Shukla, who urged him to address the plight of Indigo farmers. Mahatma Gandhi organised the Kheda Satyagraha (1918) in Gujarat. Gandhi also led the Ahmedabad Mill Strike (1918) after returning from South Africa. The Bardoli Satyagraha (1928) was led by Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel for the farmers of Bardoli.

questions.question: Who was the Viceroy of India when Mahatma Gandhi started Dandi March on 12 March 1930?
questions.explanation: Lor d Ir win (1926-1931). Other Events During his tenure: Simon Commission to India (1927); Harcourt Butler Indian States Commission (1927); Nehru Report (1928); Deepavali Declaration (1929); Lahore session of the Congress (Purna Swaraj Resolution) 1929; First Round Table Conference (1930); Gandhi-Irwin Pact (1931). Mahatma Gandhi led the Dandi March (Salt March) in 1930, accompanied by a group of 78 followers, as a nonviolent protest against the British salt monopoly.

questions.question: In which year was the Gandhi-Irwin Pact signed ?
questions.explanation: 1931. The Gandhi-Irwin Pact was signed on March 5, 1931, between Mahatma Gandhi and Lord Irwin, the Viceroy of India (1926-1931). The pact ended the Civil Disobedience Movement, initiated by Gandhi with the Salt March, and was signed before the Second Round Table Conference in London.

questions.question: In the second Round Table Conference, Mahatma Gandhi represented which of the following parties?
questions.explanation: Indian National Congr ess. Mahatma Gandhi attended only the Second Round Table Conference in London in September 1931, presided over by Ramsay MacDonald. The Conference in London was inconclusive, so Gandhiji returned to India and resumed civil disobedience. Dr. B.R. Ambedkar and Tej Bahadur Sapru attended all three Round Table Conferences.

questions.question: In which of the following placesdid Mahatma Gandhi, along with other Indians, establish the Natal Congress to ﬁght against racial discrimination?
questions.explanation: South Africa. The Natal Indian Congress, founded by Mahatma Gandhi in 1894, aimed to combat discrimination against Indians in South Africa. Gandhiji, born on 2 October 1869, returned to India on 9 January 1915.

questions.question: Which important event that started in 1930 played a signi ﬁ cant role in motivating and mobilizing Indians to join the non - violent movement for independence?
questions.explanation: Salt Saty agr aha. The Dandi March, or Salt Satyagraha, was initiated by Mahatma Gandhi from Sabarmati Ashram on March 12, 1930, and lasted until April 5, 1930, reaching Dandi (Navsari district). C. Rajagopalachari organized the Vedaranyam Salt March along the east coast, while Gandhi led the march on the west coast. The protest was against the British salt tax, and Gandhi de ﬁ ed it by collecting natural salt from the shore. In the Salt Satyagraha, Sarojini Naidu persuaded Mahatma Gandhi to allow women to join the movement.

questions.question: Which of the following leaders had gone to Lucknow to invite Mahatma Gandhi to see the plight of the peasants in Champaran?
questions.explanation: Raj K umar Shukla. The local Congress leaders Brajkishore Prasad and Raj Kumar Shukla ﬁrst met Mahatma Gandhi at the Lucknow session of the Indian National Congress in 1916 and invited Mahatma Gandhi to have ﬁrst-hand experience of the plight of Champaran peasants. Gandhi arrived in Champaran on April 15, 1917, to address the grievances of the peasants who were forced to grow indigo under oppressive conditions.

questions.question: Which of the following Pacts succeeded the Communal Award of 1932?
questions.explanation: Gandhi-A mbedkar P act. The Communal Award was announced by the British Prime Minister, Ramsay MacDonald, on 16 August 1932, based on the report of the Indian Franchise Committee (Lothian Committee). This award accorded separate electorates for Muslims, Europeans, Sikhs, Indian Christians, Anglo-Indians, Depressed classes (78 seats), and even to the Marathas for some seats in Bombay. Gandhi - Ambedkar Pact is also known as the Poona Pact. Madan Mohan Malaviya signed it on behalf of Gandhi. It provided for the Joint electorate, reserved seats (147) for depressed classes and 18% seats reserved for the central legislature.

questions.question: Mahatma Gandhi called a halt to the Non-Cooperation Movement after the Chauri Chaura incident. Where among the following places is Chauri Chaura located?
questions.explanation: Gor akhpur . Chauri Chaura, a town in Uttar Pradesh was the site of a violent clash on February 4, 1922, during the Non-Cooperation Movement. Protestors set a police station on ﬁr e, resulting in the deaths of 22 policemen inside.

questions.question: The result of which of the following movements/satyagrahas was the abolishment of the Tinkathia System under which the farmers were asked to cultivate indigo in 3/20thof their holdings ?
questions.explanation: Champar an Saty agr aha (1917) was Gandhi's ﬁrst protest in India against forced indigo farming. Brajkishore Prasad and Raj Kumar Shukla ﬁrst met Mahatma Gandhi at the Lucknow session of the Indian National Congress in 1916 and invited him to have ﬁrst-hand experience of the plight of Champaran peasants.

questions.question: Which of the following options is not related to the non-cooperation movement started by Mahatma Gandhi?

options.a: Signing of the resolution of Poorna Swaraj in Lahore

questions.explanation: The resolution for Poorna Swaraj (complete independence) was passed at the Lahore session of the Indian National Congress in December 1929, under the leadership of Jawaharlal Nehru. He hoisted the Indian tricolor ﬂag on the banks of the Ravi river. The non-cooperation movement was launched by Mahatma Gandhi in 1920 to persuade Indians to withdraw their cooperation from the British government and advocate for self-governance.

questions.question: Gandhiji gave his slogan of ‘Do or Die’ during which of the following Movements/Satyagrahas?
questions.explanation: The Quit India Mo v ement (also known as the August Movement) was launched by the Indian National Congress on August 8, 1942. Other key movements by Mahatma Gandhi include the Civil Disobedience Movement (1930), the Kheda Satyagraha (1918) in Gujarat, demanding relaxation in revenue collection for peasants, and the Champaran Satyagraha (1917), opposing the Tinkathia system, which forced peasants to grow indigo on 3/20th of their land.

questions.question: Vallabhbhai Patel and ________ helped Gandhi in Kheda by organising his tour of the villages and urging the peasants to stand ﬁrm against the government.
questions.explanation: Indulal Y ajnik. Mahatma Gandhi returned to India on 9 January 1915.

questions.question: On whose advice did Gandhi spend a year traveling around British India to know the people of India?
questions.explanation: Gopal Krishna Gokhale. He was a mentor to both Mahatma Gandhi and Mohammed Ali Jinnah.

questions.question: In which year was Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi born?
questions.explanation: 1869. Mahatma Gandhi: Born on 2nd October (observed as International Day of Non-violence). He came back to India on 9th January (celebrated as ‘NRI day’ or ‘Pravasi Bhartiya Divas’ since 2003) 1915 from South Africa.

questions.question: Who was the founder of Harijan Sevak Sangh founded in 1932?
questions.explanation: Mahatma Gandhi . Organisation Founded by MK Gandhi: Majdoor Mahajan (1916) in Ahmedabad. It was related to the labour movement or textile movement in Gujarat. All-India Spinners Association (1925), All-India Village Industries Association (1934).

questions.question: The famous scientist, Albert Einstein was inspired by whose idea on non-violence?
questions.explanation: Mahatma Gandhi. His Ideas - Truth and nonviolence, Satyagraha, Sarvodaya (Progress of All), Swaraj (self-rule), Trusteeship (Wealthy people to look after the welfare of the people).

questions.question: Who among the following returned his/her medal of Kaisar-i-Hind in 1920 ?
questions.explanation: Mahatma Gandhi. Kaiser - e - Hind title was given to Mahatma Gandhi by Governor-General of India Lord Hardinge in 1915 for his services during the Boer War (1899 - 1902). Gandhi returned his title in response to the Jallianwala Bagh Massacr e (13th April 1919). Rabindranath Tagore returned his Knighthood title in response to the massacre.Q.648. In 1918, Mahatma Gandhi successfully led a strike of ________. SSC MTS 09/05/2023 (Evening) (a) Army Soldiers (b) Dalits (c) Mill workers (d) Traders Sol.648.(c) Mill work ers. Ahmedabad Mill Strik e (1918) -This is considered theﬁrst hunger strike led by Gandhi.

questions.question: In Kheda, Gujarat, Patidar peasants organised non-violent campaigns against which of the following demand of the British?
questions.explanation: High land r e v enue. Kheda Satyagraha of 1918 was a satyagraha movement in the Kheda district of Gujarat in India organized by Mahatma Gandhi during the period of the British Raj. The peasant-Patidar community of Kheda, who refused to accept a 23 percent tax increase imposed on them despite a devastating crop failure and an outbreak of plague and cholera, served as the focal point of this satyagraha.

questions.question: When did depressed classes get the reserved seats in provincial and central legislative councils, but they were to be voted in by the General electorate?
questions.explanation: September 1932. Poona Pact - Agreement between Mahatma Gandhi and Dr. B. R. Ambedkar - Seats reserved for the depressed classes were increased from 71 to 147 in provincial legislatures and 18 percent of the total in the Central Legislature.

questions.question: Gandhiji, aged ______, arrived in India in 1915 from South Africa.
questions.explanation: 46. Gandhiji (born on 2nd October 1869 in Porbandar, Gujarat) went to South Africa in 1893 as a lawyer, settling ﬁrst in Durban before moving to Johannesburg.

questions.question: Which of the following Indian freedom ﬁghter said that "Be the change you wish to see in the world" ?
questions.explanation: Mahatma Gandhi. His other Famous Quotes: “A man is but the product of his thoughts. What he thinks, he becomes.”, “Nobody can hurt me without my permission.”

questions.question: In which of the following years, did Subhas Chandra Bose refer to Mahatma Gandhi as the “Father of the Nation”?
questions.explanation: 1944. Subhas Chandra Bose called Mahatma Gandhi the ‘Father of the Nation’ in a radio address from Singapore on 6 July 1944.

questions.question: The All-India Khilafat Conference in November 1919 took place at:
questions.explanation: Delhi. In early 1919, the All India Khilafat Committee was formed under the leadership of the Ali brothers (Maulana Muhammad Ali and Shaukat Ali), Maulana Abul Kalam Azad, Ajmal Khan and Hasrat Mohani, to force the British Government to change its attitude to Turkey.

questions.question: Who among the following called Netaji Subhash Chandra Bose a patriot of patriots?
questions.explanation: Mahatma Gandhi. Rabindranath Tagore called Subhash Chandra Bose a 'Desh Nayak'. The famous slogan given by Subhas Chandra Bose: “Dilli Chalo, Jai Hind”, "Give me blood, I will give freedom". Subhas Chandra Bose Autobiography: “The Indian Struggle”.

questions.question: Which of the following events occurred as an after-effect of the Jallianwala Bagh Massacre (13th April 1919) ?

options.a: Hunger strike to settle disputes between the mill owners and the workers
options.b: Individual princes could sign a separate agreement with the British
options.c: Hunter Commission was appointed by the British Government
options.d: Popularisation of Charkha and Khadi and Jail Bharo by Congress volunteers.

questions.explanation: The Hunter Commission came to India in 1919 under the leadership of William Hunter to investigate the Jallianwala Bagh massacre. Another Hunter Commission was on Indian Education, prepared in 1882 by William Wilson Hunter.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a reason for slowing down the Non-cooperation Movement?

options.a: Due to the absence of alternative Indian institution, boycott of British Institutions posed a problem.
options.b: Leaders of the movement shifted their focus towards domestic work thus created a void.
options.c: Many lawyers resigned but then absence of income for a longer period of time compelled them to rejoin.
options.d: Khadi cloth was often more expensive than mass-produced mill cloth and poor people could not afford to buy it.

questions.explanation: Mahatma Gandhi started the Non- Cooperation Movement on 4 September 1920. Lord Chelmsford was the viceroy of British India during the Non-Cooperation Movement. The Chauri -Chaura incident was the driver of subsequent withdrawal of the Non -Cooperation Movement.

questions.question: In which of the following years was the civil disobedience campaign completely Ceased ?
questions.explanation: 1934 . It began with the famous Dandi March of Gandhiji, on 12 March 1930.

questions.question: Who among the following freedom ﬁghters hoisted the Indian ﬂag at the Gowalia Tank Maidan in Mumbai during the Quit India Movement ?
questions.explanation: Aruna Asaf Ali. Aruna Asaf Ali popularly known as the 'Grand Old Lady' of the Independence Movement.

questions.question: Who among the following was the only martyr of the Quit India phase of the freedom movement who was hanged ?
questions.explanation: K ushal K onwar. Facts of Quit India Movement - The Quit India Resolution was passed by the Congress Working Committee on 8 August 1942 in Bombay. ‘Quit India’ or ‘Bharat Chodo 'slogan was given in this movement . Gandhiji gave the slogan to the people – ‘Do or die’; It is also known as the India August Movement or August Kranti.

questions.question: Which Newspaper was founded by Mahatma Gandhi in South Africa in 1903?
questions.explanation: Indian Opinion . The Indian Opinion was an important newspaper established in 1903 in Durban, South Africa, by Mahatma Gandhi and M. H. Nazar. It was a primary communication organ of the Natal Indian Congress.

questions.question: C Rajagopalachari led the Salt Satyagraha in which of the following states ?
questions.explanation: T amil Nadu. Salt March (Dandi Satyagraha, 12th March 1930 to 6th April, 1930 ) was an act of nonviolent civil disobedience led by Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi. Other leaders - K. Kelappan (Malabar), Gopabandhu Chaudhary (Orissa). Expansion of British Rule Q.663 . Which of the following personalities had the key role in ﬁghting against the British East India Company in the Battle of Porto Novo? SSC MTS 28/10/2024 (2nd Shift) (a) Hyder Ali (b) Maharaja Ranjit Singh (c) Alamgir-II (d) Gopala Nayaker Sol.663.(a) Hy der Ali. The Battle of Porto Novo (1781) was fought between Hyder Ali and the East India Company (EIC), led by Sir Eyre Coote. Hyder Ali was a key ﬁgur e in the Anglo-Mysore Wars. The First Anglo-Mysore War (1767–1769) was fought between Hyder Ali and the EIC. The Second Anglo-Mysore War (1780–1784) also saw Hyder Ali leading the Mysore forces; however, he died during the con ﬂ ict, after which his son, Tipu Sultan, continued the war.

questions.question: Which of the following battles is considered the formal beginning of the British Raj in India?
questions.explanation: The Battle of Plasse y took place in Bengal on June 23, 1757. British East India Company troops, led by Robert Clive, confronted the forces of Siraj-ud-Daulah, the Nawab of Bengal, along with his French allies. The British East India Company won this battle.

questions.question: In which year was the battle of Buxar fought?
questions.explanation: 1764. Battle of Buxar : Fought between the British East India Company and the combined armies of Mir Qasim (Nawab of Bengal), Shuja-ud-Daulah (Nawab of Awadh), and the Mughal Emperor, Shah Alam II. The British East India Company was led by Hector Munro. The Britishers won this battle. The Mughal Emperor Shah Alam II - granted the Diwani of Bengal, Bihar and Orissa to the East India Company.

questions.question: Where was the ﬁrst jute mill set up in India?
questions.explanation: Rishr a (West Bengal, 1855). First Jute mill (Acland Mill) - Established by British entrepreneur George Acland and Bengali ﬁnancier Babu By Sumber Sen in Bengal Presidency. Central Research Institute for Jute and Allied Fibers is located in West Bengal. National jute Board formed by National Jute board Act, 2008 under the Ministry of Textile. In 1854, the ﬁrst modern cotton mill was established in Mumbai by Cowaszee Nanabhoy Davar.

questions.question: Which of the following modern industries were in operation in India during the British Rule ? a. Cotton b. Jute c. Iron and Steel

options.a: All of a, b and c

questions.explanation: All of a, b and c. British rulers never permitted modernization nor did they encourage the growth of industries. The unbalanced and lopsided growth structures were a legacy of British rule inIndia. The ﬁrst iron and steel mill was established in 1907, in Jamshedpur by Tata Iron and Steel Company.

questions.question: In the 18thcentury, the development of cotton industries in Britain resulted in _________ .

options.a: Increase in demand of Indian textile in Britain
options.b: Decline of textiles production in India
options.c: Increase in demand of Indian textile in India
options.d: Decline of British textiles industries in India

questions.explanation: Reason for decline: Export of textiles to England became more and more di ﬃ cult because the British Government imposed high duties on Indian textiles. Thorp Mill (1764) - The ﬁrst water-powered cotton mill in the world was constructed at Royton, Lancashire (England). The ﬁrst cotton textile mill (India) - Established at Fort Glastor near Kolkata (1818).

questions.question: In 1600, the East India company acquired a charter from_________.
questions.explanation: Queen Elizabeth I. By the Charter (1600 A.D), they got the sole right to trade with the East, without competition from other British traders. The Company's ships ﬁrst arrived in India, at the port of Surat, in 1608.

questions.question: The ﬁrst English factory was set up on the banks of the river _______ in 1651.
questions.explanation: Hugli. First English factory (Temporary) in India - Masulipattam (1611). First English factory (Permanent) in India - Surat (1613). First factory of other European Companies in India: Dutch - Masulipatnam (Andhra Pradesh) in 1605, Portuguese - Calicut (Kerala) in 1500, French - Surat (Gujarat) in 1668.

questions.question: Which of the following pairs is correct regarding the East India Company army? I. Sawar — Men on horses Il. Musket — A heavy gun used by infantry soldiers
questions.explanation: Both l and II.Q.672. The British East India Company captured Pondicherry (Puducherry) from the French in the year ______. SSC CGL 11/04/2022 (Morning) (a) 1761 (b) 1699 (c) 1674 (d) 1738 Sol.672.(a) 1761 . Pondicherry got its freedom from the French on 1st November 1954. The Union Territory of Puducherry comprises the erstwhile French colonies viz. Puducherry, Karaikal, Mahe and Yanam.

questions.question: Which monument was built in 1924 to welcome King George V and Queen Mary?
questions.explanation: Gatewa y of India. The Gateway of India is an Arch-Monument built in the early 20th century in the city of Mumbai.

questions.question: Tirot Sing was the leader of which of the following revolts?
questions.explanation: Khasi uprising. Tirot Sing, also known as U Tirot Sing Syiem, was a Khasi chief in the early 19th century. The Khasi uprising (Anglo-Khasi War) was a conﬂ ict between the Khasi people and the British Empire that took place from 1829 to 1833. The Sanyasi Uprisings took place in Bengal between the periods of 1770- 1820s. Poligar Revolt fought between the British and the Poligars, feudal lords in southern India. The Singpho Rebellion of 1830 took place in Assam.

questions.question: The planned large-scale disruption of rail communications and the seizure of arms in Bengal were undertaken under the leadership of _____.
questions.explanation: Jatin Mukherjee , also known as Bagha Jatin, was a prominent revolutionary leader in Bengal during the early 20th century who fought against British colonial rule. He was a key member of the Anushilan Samiti, a revolutionary organization that planned large-scale armed resistance against the British.

questions.question: Under the leadership of which of the following revolutionaries was the Chittagong Armoury Raid conducted?
questions.explanation: Sur y a Sen is also known as Master Da. He founded The Indian Republican Army. The Chittagong Armoury Raid took place on April 18, 1930, in Chittagong (now in Bangladesh). Ras Bihari Bose was the founder of the 'Indian Independence League’. He chaired the Indian Independence League in 1942 to formally launch the Indian National Army.

questions.question: Sido and Kanhu were leaders of which of the following tribal rebellions?
questions.explanation: Santhal Rebellion (Hul revolt) began in 1855, led by Sidhu, Kanhu, Chand, Bhairav, and their sisters Phulo and Jhano. The Malabar rebellion (Moplah rebellion) was an armed uprising by the Mappila Muslims of Kerala in 1921. The Kol rebellion (1829-39) was led by Buddhu Bhagat, Madara Mahato, and Joa Bhagat in the Chota Nagpur region. The Munda Rebellion, led by Birsa Munda, took place in the Ranchi area in 1899-1900.

questions.question: In which of the following years was VD Savarkar sent to Andaman Jail by the British?
questions.explanation: 1911. Vinayak Damodar Savarkar was sent to the Cellular Jail on the punishment of 'Kala Pani’ under the Nashik Conspiracy Case for the murder of the Collector of Nashik District, Jackson. While serving his sentence in the Cellular Jail, he wrote the epic 'Kamala' on the prison walls.

questions.question: In 1910, The United India House at Seattle, USA, was set up by _________.

options.b: Tarak Nath Das and GD Kumar

questions.explanation: T ar ak Nath Das and GD K umar . They were Indian revolutionarieswho collaborated to promote Indian independence from British rule. Shyamji Krishna Varma founded the Indian Home Rule Society, India House, and The Indian Sociologist in London. Vinayak Damodar Savarkar and his brother Ganesh Damodar Savarkar established the Abhinav Bharat Society, also known as the Young India Society, in 1904. Q.680 . Which of the following groups/parties was started in San Francisco, United States of America during the Indian National Movement? SSC CGL 26/09/2024 (2nd Shift) (a) Hindustan Socialist Republican Association (b) Swaraj Party (c) Khilafat Committee (d) Ghadar Party Sol.680.(d) Ghadar P ar ty : Founded in 1913 in the U.S. as the Hindi Association of the Paci ﬁ c Coast, it aimed to free India from British rule. It was founded by Sohan Singh Bhakna and Lala Har Dayal.

questions.question: Bankim Chandra Chatterjee wrote a novel Anand Math based on which of the following rebellions/revolts?
questions.explanation: Sany asi Rebellion. The Sanyasi Uprisings took place in Bengal between the periods of 1770- 1820s. The Sanyasis rose in rebellion after the great famine of 1770 in Bengal which caused acute chaos and misery. However, the immediate cause of the rebellion was the restrictions imposed by the British upon pilgrims visiting holy places among both Hindus and Muslims. The national song of India, 'Vande Mataram’, is an excerpt taken from the novel Anand Math written by Bankim Chandra Chattopadhyay.

questions.question: Who founded the Bombay Presidency Association in 1885 along with Pherozeshah Mehta and KT Telang?
questions.explanation: Badruddin T y abji was the third President (in 1887) of the Indian National Congress and the ﬁrst Muslim to hold that position. Other Political organisations and founder: London Indian Society (1865) - Dadabhai Naoroji. East India Association (1866) - Dadabhai Naoroji. Poona Sarvajanik Sabha (1870) - G.V. Joshi, S.H. Sathe,S.H. Chiplonkar and Mahadev Govind Ranade.

questions.question: What was the name of the campaign led by the Ali brothers and Maulana Abul Kalam Azad to save the Ottoman Caliphate as a symbol of unity among the Muslim community?
questions.explanation: Khilafat Mo v ement. It was a political campaign that took place from 1919 to 1922 in British India. It was a protest against the British government's policies towards Turkey and the planned dismantling of the Ottoman Empire after World War I. The Faraizi movement was started by Haji Shariatullah in 1818. The Ahmadiyya movement was founded by Mirza Ghulam Ahmad in 1889. Syed Ahmed Barelvi started the Wahabi Movement in the 1820s.

questions.question: Who was executed as an accomplice to the murder of AMT Jackson ?
questions.explanation: Krishnaji Gopal Kar v e. Jackson murder case also known as Nasik Conspiracy (Year 1909). Anant Laxman Kanhere shot Jackson, the Collector of Nashik, at Vijayanand theatre. Kanhere was a member of the Abhinav Bharat society. The event led to the arrest of Savarkar. Abhinav Bharat Society was a secret society founded by Vinayak Damodar Savarkar and his brother Ganesh Damodar Savarkar in 1904.

questions.question: Which of the following revolutionists was associated with the Anushilan Samiti?
questions.explanation: Jatindr a Mohan Sengupta. Anushilan Samiti was founded by Satish Chandra Bose and Barrister Praanath Mitra. Bhagat Singh, Rajguru, and Sukhdev were members of the Hindustan Socialist Republican Association (HSRA), involved in the Lahore Conspiracy Case of 1929.

questions.question: In 1908 Khudiram Bose along with _________ was involved in throwing a bomb at a carriage believing it to be occupied by Kingsford, the then sitting judge of Muzaffarpur.
questions.explanation: Pr afulla Chaki was an Indian revolutionary associated with the Jugantar group of revolutionaries. Khudiram Bose was among the youngest martyrs of India's freedom struggle against the British. At just 15, he had joined Anushilan Samiti and took part in several revolutionary activities against the British Raj. He was executed on August 11, 1908, in Muzaffarpur jail in Bihar.

questions.question: During British rule in India, who among the following unfurled the ﬂag of India in Germany in 1907?
questions.explanation: Madam Bhikaji Cama. She founded the ‘Paris Indian Society’. Annie Besant (British socialist) - Founded Home rule league in India and she was ﬁrst woman president of Indian National Congress (INC). Sarojini Naidu - First Indian woman president of the INC. Sucheta Kriplani holds the distinction of being India's ﬁrst female Chief Minister, serving the state of Uttar Pradesh.

questions.question: Who among the following revolutionaries was one of the co-founders of the Hindustan Socialist Republican Association (HSRA) which got established in 1928 at Ferozeshah Kotla in Delhi?
questions.explanation: Bhagat Singh. The Hindustan Socialist Republican Association (HSRA), formerly known as the Hindustan Republican Army (HRA) was founded by Ram Prasad Bismil, Sachindra Nath Bakshi, Sachindranath Sanyal, and Jogesh Chandra Chatterjee. HRA was established in October 1924 in Kanpur, Uttar Pradesh, by revolutionaries including Ram Prasad Bismil, Jogesh Chatterjee, Chandrashekhar Azad, Yogendra Shukla, and Sachindranath Sanyal. HRA took to socialist ideas, under Chandrashekhar Azad leadership, in 1928.Q.689. Who among the following founded the Khudai Khidmatgars or the Red Shirts, a powerful non-violent movement? Graduate Level 26/06/2024 (Shift - 3) (a) Khan Abdul Ghaffar Khan (b) BR Ambedkar (c) Subhas Chandra Bose (d) Mahatma Gandhi Sol.689.(a) Khan Abdul Ghaffar Khan, also known as 'Frontier Gandhi' and 'Bacha Khan', was a prominent freedom ﬁghter and advocate of non-violence. He became the ﬁrst non-Indian to receive Bharat Ratna in 1987. B.R. Ambedkar was the ﬁrst Law Minister of India and the architect of the Indian Constitution. Subhas Chandra Bose was an Indian nationalist and the founder of the All India Forward Bloc. Mahatma Gandhi was an Indian lawyer and anti-colonial nationalist, renowned for his principles of non-violence.

questions.question: The 'Servants of India Society' was the brainchild of which of the following leaders ?
questions.explanation: Gopal Krishna Gokhale founded - Ranade Institute of Economics (1908). He started an English weekly newspaper, The Hitavada (The people's paper). Servants of India Society was founded in 1905 .Chitranjan Das is popularly known as Deshbandhu. Lala Lajpat Rai - Punjab National bank.

questions.question: ‘Mitra Mela’ was a revolutionary organisation founded by:
questions.explanation: Vina y ak Sa v arkar . Mitra Mela was founded in the year 1899 in Nashik. He and his brother Ganesh Damodar established the society (later renamed Abhinav Bharat Society or the Young India Society). It believed in the concept of Armed rebellion to overthrow the British.

questions.question: On 8th April 1929, who among the following two revolutionists threw a bomb in the Central Legislative Assembly?

options.b: Bhagat Singh and Ram Prasad Bismil

questions.explanation: Bhagat Singh and Batuk eshwar Dutt. They threw bomb against the passage of the Public Safety Bill and the Trade Disputes Bill which would reduce the civil liberties of citizens. They were members of the Hindustan Socialist Republican Association. On 23rd March, 1931 Bhagat Singh was hanged in the Lahore jail at the age of 23. Q.693 . Who is regarded as the 'Mother of Indian Revolution' ? SSC MTS 06/09/2023 (2nd Shift) (a) Annie Besant (b) Usha Mehta (c) Madame Cama (d) Kasturba Gandhi Sol.693.(c) Madame Cama : She is famous for hosting the precursor to the Indian National Flag on foreign soil (Stuttgart in Germany).

questions.question: Which great leader died due to his injuries in the Anti-Simon Commission Protest of 1928?
questions.explanation: Lala Lajpat Rai (Freedom ﬁghters). He was also called ‘Punjab Kesari’ and 'Lion of Punjab'. Bal Gangadhar Tilak - Founded the Home Rule League in 1916. Simon Commission - Indian Statutory Commission, group of seven Members, not a single Indian member in it, Headed by Sir John Simon. Simon Commission came to India in 1928.

questions.question: Who among the following, along with her close associates, started the secret congress radio during the Quit India Movement?
questions.explanation: Usha Mehta. Awards - Padma Vibhushan (1998).

questions.question: The statement- "We shall die to awaken the nation" was made by.
questions.explanation: Bagha Jatin (Jatindranath Mukherjee) - Born on 7th December 1879 in Kayagram (Nadia district) present-day Bangladesh. He acquired the moniker ‘Bagha Jatin’ when he single-handedly killed a Bengal tiger in 1906. Bir Tikendrajit Singh - He was a prince of the independent Kingdom of Manipur.

questions.question: Which freedom ﬁghter was involved in the Kakori Train Action in 1925, for which the British government condemned him to death?
questions.explanation: Ram Pr asad Bismil. Kakori T r ain Action - It was a train robbery (part of Kakori Conspiracy) that took place on 9 August 1925, near Lucknow. It was organised by the Hindustan Republican Association (HRA). Ram Prasad Bismil, Ashfaqulla Khan, and Roshan Singh were hanged on 19 December 1927. Rajendra Nath Lahiri hanged on December 17, 1927.

questions.question: The Revolutionary Socialist Party was established in ________.
questions.explanation: 19 Mar ch 1940 by Tridib Chaudhuri. It has its roots in the Bengali liberation movement Anushilan Samiti and the Hindustan Socialist Republican Association . The ﬁrst general secretary of the party was Jogesh Chandra Chatterjee.

questions.question: Who said the following words? “Sarfaroshi ki tamanna ab hamare dil me hai, dekhna hai zor kitna baazu-e-qaatil mein hai”
questions.explanation: Rampr asad Bismil (poet, writer, freedom ﬁghter) - He participated in the Mainpuri Conspiracy of 1918, and the Kakori Conspiracy of 1925. Subhash Chandra Bose - “Jai Hind” & “Tum Mujhe Khoon Do, Mai Tumhe Azadi Dunga”. Mahatma Gandhi - “Karo ya Maro”. Muhammad Iqbal - “Sare Jahan Se Achha Hindustan Hamara”. Bankim Chandra Chatterjee - “Vande Mataram”. Pandit Madan Mohan Malviya - “Satyameva Jayate”.

questions.question: Which of the following revolutionaries stated during his trial that "he did not wish to glorify the cult of the bomb and pistol but wanted a revolution in society"?
questions.explanation: Bhagat Singh and Batukeshwar Dutt threw a bomb in the Central Legislative Assembly on 8 April 1929. Sachindra Nath Sanyal was co-founder of the Hindustan Republican Association (HRA) in 1924. Barindra Kumar Ghosh (younger brother of Sri Aurobindo) published the Bengali weekly Jugantar patrika (1906).

questions.question: Who among the following revolutionaries was associated with Swadesh Bandhav Samiti?
questions.explanation: Ashwini K umar Dutta founded the Swadesh Bandhab Samiti on 6thAugust 1905 in Barisal district (Bangladesh). Weekly paper: Barisal Hitaishi. He was responsible for the creation of the Cooperative Hindustan Bank in 1908.

questions.question: Who among the following organized the armed rebellion against the British Raj in 1879 in Maharashtra?
questions.explanation: V asude v Balwant Phadk e - Known as the First Revolutionary and also the ‘Father of militant nationalism in India.’ Birsa Munda also known as Dharti Aaba (Father of Earth), belonged to the Munda tribe in the Chotanagpur Plateau area.

questions.question: The Communist Party of India was founded at ________ in October 1920.
questions.explanation: T ashk ent. M.N. Roy (Manabendra Nath Roy), Abani Mukherji, founded the Communist Party of India (CPI) in 1920 in Tashkent (now the capital of Uzbekistan). The Communist Party of India was o ﬃ cially formed (1925) in Kanpur. Its founders included M. N. Roy, his wife Evelyn Trent, Abani Mukherji, and M. P . T. Acharya (Mandayam Parthasarathi Tirumal Acharya).Q.704. The Koraput Revolution occurred in ______ during the Quit India Movement. Graduate Level 02/08/2022 (Shift - 3) (a) Orissa (b) the United Provinces (c) Bihar (d) Bengal Sol.704.(a) Odisha. Koraput revolution {On August 25, 1942, 19 people died on the spot in police ﬁring at Paparandi in Nabarangpur (Odisha). More than a thousand were jailed in Koraput district (Odisha). Veer Lakhan Nayak (Gandhi of Odisha) was hanged}. Quit India movement (8 August 1942 - 1944) - led by Mahatma Gandhi.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about Bharat Naujawan Sabha is INCORRECT?

options.b: Bhagat Singh became its founding secretary
options.d: It started political work among the youth, peasants, and workers

questions.explanation: Bharat Naujawan Sabha was founded in Lahore (1926) by Bhagat Singh.

questions.question: Who among the following led the famous Indigo Revolt in Bengal?

options.b: Bishnu Charan Biswas and Digambar Biswas

questions.explanation: Digambar Biswas and Bishnu Biswas of Nadia district Bengal led the Indigo revolt. This Revolt began as a nonviolent strike in March 1859, as the ryots of a village in Bengal's Nadia district all agreed to refuse to grow any more indigo.

questions.question: Which of the following revolutionaries was arrested by the British as an accused in the Alipore Bomb Conspiracy Case?
questions.explanation: Kanailal Dutta was among the 33 revolutionaries who got arrested in the Emperor vs Aurobindo Ghosh (Alipore Bomb Case). The Alipore Bomb case was a historic trial in which the British Government tried to implicate Sri Aurobindo in various revolutionary activities.

questions.question: At which of the following jails was Khudiram Bose sent to the gallows?
questions.explanation: Muzaffarpur . Khudiram Bose was arrested for trying to assassinate British judge Douglas Kingsford, he was executed on August 11, 1908, in Muzaffarpur jail in Bihar.

questions.question: Who among the following persons was associated with the Chittagong Armoury Raid?
questions.explanation: Ar dhendu Dastidar .

questions.question: Who among the following killed Michael O'Dwyer, the British o ﬃ cer responsible for the Jallianwala Bagh massacre?
questions.explanation: Udham Singh. O'Dwyer, was shot dead at a joint meeting of the East India Association and the Central Asian Society in Caxton Hall, in retaliation for the massacre in Jallianwala Bagh Amritsar.

questions.question: Who among the following assassinated Sir William Hutt Curzon Wyllie in London?
questions.explanation: Madan Lal Dhingr a was an Indian revolutionary independence activist. While studying in England, he assassinated Sir William Hutt Curzon Wyllie, a British o ﬃ cial, cited as one of the ﬁrst acts of revolution in the Indian independence movement in the 20th century.

questions.question: Veer Surendra Sai was a freedom ﬁghter from:
questions.explanation: Odisha . Veer Surendra Sai started his revolt against British rule long before 1857 and continued his guerrilla war against the British authority from 1857 till 1864 long after 1857 thestruggle led by the Rani of Jhansi, Tantia Tope, Kunwar Singh and others had subsided.

questions.question: Who was the ﬁrst general secretary of the All India Farmer’s Congress?
questions.explanation: NG Ranga. He was a freedom ﬁghter from Andhra Pradesh and also received Padma Vibhusan from the President of India for his contributions to the peasants' movement.

questions.question: Who among the following was a leader who led a revolt against British rule from North East India?
questions.explanation: Rani Gaidinliu was a Naga spiritual and political leader who led a revolt against British rule in India. At the age of 13, she joined the Heraka religious movement established by her cousin Haipou Jadonang in Manipur.

questions.question: Where of the following was Bal Gangadhar Tilak’s Home Rule Movement active?
questions.explanation: Mahar ashtr a. Bal Gangadhar Tilak launched his Home Rule Movement in 1916. It was primarily centered in Maharashtra, which was his home base. He had signi ﬁ cant inﬂ uence and following in this region. He published his ideas through his newspapers "Kesari" (in Marathi) and "Mahratta" (in English) from Maharashtra.

questions.question: Which of the following organisations, divided into two branches, was established by Lokmanya Bal Gangadhar Tilak and Annie Besant?
questions.explanation: Home Rule League. In April1916, Tilak founded the Indian Home Rule League, with its headquarters in Poona. The league's ﬁrst meeting was held in Belgaum. In September 1916, Annie Besant founded the All-India Home Rule League, based in Madras. Organizations and their founders : Indian Association - Surendranath Banerjee and Ananda Mohan Bose; Theosophical Society - Helena Blavatsky, Henry Steel Olcott, William Quan Judge.

questions.question: Which of the following events took place in the United Provinces in February, 1922?
questions.explanation: Chauri Chaur a Incident took place at Chauri Chaura in the Gorakhpur district of United Provinces (now Uttar Pradesh) in British India. The police there ﬁr ed upon a large group of protesters participating in the non-cooperation movement. In retaliation, the demonstrators attacked and set ﬁr e to a police station, killing all of its occupants. The incident led to the deaths of three civilians and 22 policemen. Mahatma Gandhi halted the non-cooperation movement on 12th February 1922 after the Chauri Chaura incident.

questions.question: Indian National Army was formed in which of the following countries?
questions.explanation: Singapor e. The Indian National Army (INA), also known as the Azad Hind Fauj, was a collaborationist armed unit of Indian supporters that fought under the command of the Japanese Empire. It was founded by Mohan Singh in September 1942 during World War II. Q.719 . Being the President of the Indian National Congress, who among the following had called for complete independence from the British Raj in 1929? SSC CGL 23/09/2024 (2nd Shift) (a) Jawaharlal Nehru (b) Subhas Chandra Bose (c) Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel (d) Mahatma Gandhi Sol.719.(a) Jawaharlal Nehru. He was an Indian freedom ﬁghter who later became the ﬁrst Prime Minister of India. In December 1929, he was chosen as the President of the Indian National Congress during its annual session in Lahore. At this event, he raised the tricolour ﬂag on the banks of the Ravi River.

questions.question: On 31 October 1940, who among the following was arrested for offering individual Satyagraha to protest against India’s forced participation in the Second World War ?
questions.explanation: Jawaharlal Nehru. On 31 October 1940, Jawaharlal Nehru was arrested for participating in individual Satyagraha, protesting against India’s forced involvement in the Second World War. During this period, Nehru also engaged in limited Civil Disobedience and spent approximately nine years in jail between 1921 and 1945.

questions.question: Who among the following was primarily an integral part of the Indian National Army (INA)?
questions.explanation: Subhas Chandr a Bose. Indian National Army (INA) was a collaborationist armed unit of Indian collaborators that fought under the command of the Japanese Empire. It was founded by Mohan Singh in September 1942 in Southeast Asia during World War II. Some notable people associated: Mohan Singh, Habib ur Rahman, Lt. Col Hori Lal Verma.

questions.question: What is the name of the famous British social reformer known for founding the Indian Home Rule League in 1916 ?
questions.explanation: A nnie Besant. Annie Besant, an Irish theosophist, started the Home Rule movement in India in 1916, demanding self-government similar to the Irish Home Rule League. She founded the Home Rule League in Madras in September 1916 and established over 200 branches across India.

questions.question: Who among the following personalities renounced his knighthood after the Jallianwala Bagh massacre?
questions.explanation: Rabindr anath T agor e. The Jallianwala Bagh massacre occurred on April 13, 1919, when British troops, led by General Dyer, ﬁr ed on a peaceful crowd in Amritsar, killing hundreds. In protest, Mahatma Gandhi returned the Kaiser-i-Hind title in 1920. The Hunter Commission was set up in 1919 to investigate the incident.

questions.question: Who among the following was one of the founders of the Swaraj Party?
questions.explanation: Chittar anjan Das. The Swaraj Party, initially called the Congress Khilafat Swaraj Party, was established in 1923 by Chittaranjan Das and Motilal Nehru. This formation took place after the Gaya Annual Conference of the Indian National Congress in December 1922. Other important organizations and their founders: Indian National Congress (1885): Founded by A.O. Hume. All India Kisan Sabha (1936): Sahajanand Saraswati. Azad Hind Fauj (1943): Netaji Subhash Chandra Bose.

questions.question: Which of the following Satyagrahas was started by Dr B R Ambedkar in 1927?
questions.explanation: Mahad Saty agr aha: It is also known as the Chavdar Tank Satyagraha, begun in Mahad (Raigad), Maharashtra. It centered around the Chavdar Tank, a public water source that was reserved exclusively for upper castes.

questions.question: Who among the following leaders founded the women's wing of Congress in 1940 ?
questions.explanation: Sucheta Kriplani (First female Chief Minister of Uttar Pradesh from 1963 - 67). She sang Vande Mataram on 14thAugust 1947 in the opening session of Parliament.

questions.question: On 15 August 1947, the ﬁrst prime minister of India, JawaharlalNehru, raised the Indian National Flag above the __________ of Red Fort in Delhi.
questions.explanation: Lahori Gate. The National Flag of India was adopted on 22 July 1947, Designed by Pingali Venkayya.

questions.question: Who moved the resolution proposing that the National Flag of India be a 'horizontal tricolor of saffron, white and dark green in equal proportion '?
questions.explanation: Jawaharlal Nehru. The Tricolour was adopted as our national ﬂag at a Congress Committee meeting in Karachi in 1931.

questions.question: Who among the following was the founder of 'Tiger Legion' or 'Free India Legion'?
questions.explanation: Subhas Chandr a Bose - founder of 'Azad Hind Fauj'. 'Tiger Legion' was formed in 1941 with the help of the German Government to ﬁght against the British.

questions.question: The authorities of which country denied permission to land the ship Komagata Maru, carrying Indians?
questions.explanation: Canada. The Komagata Maru was a coal-transport steamship that had been converted into a passenger ship by Hong Kong-based businessman Gurdit Singh. It set off from Hong Kong in April 1914, reaching Vancouver’s harbour a month later with 376 people on board, most of them Sikhs. The steamship eventually ended up back in India on 29th Sep 1914 at Budge Budge, Calcutta.

questions.question: The origin of Directive Principles of State Policy can be traced to which of the following?
questions.explanation: Kar achi Resolution (1931) . The Karachi Session of Congress in 1931 was presided by Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel. Gandhi Irwin Pact - 5 March 1931. It marked the end of civil disobedience.

questions.question: Who among the following freedom ﬁghters was one of the founders of the Fergusson College in Pune in 1885 ?
questions.explanation: Bal Gangadhar Tilak. Other Co-founding members of Fergusson College :- Vaman Shriram Apte, Vishnushashtri Chiplunkar, Mahadeo Ballal Namjoshi and Gopal Krishna Agarkar.

questions.question: Motilal Nehru, C.R. Das gave up their legal practices during which Movement ?
questions.explanation: Non-Cooper ation Mo v ement (1920 - 1922) . It was organized by Mahatma Gandhi to obtain self governance. Many lawyers gave up their practices such as Motilal Nehru, C.Rajagopalachari, Saifuddin Kitchlew, Vallabhbhai Patel, Asaf Ali, T. Prakasam and Rajendra Prasad.

questions.question: When was Alluri Sitarama Raju arrested ending a two-year armed tribal struggle?
questions.explanation: Ma y 1924. He was publicly executed in Koyyuru on May 7 after being tied to a tree and shot. His ﬁnal resting place (tomb) is at Krishna Devi Peta, 111 km from Visakhapatnam. In August 1922, he launched the Rampa Rebellion (Manyam Rebellion) against the British. During this rebellion, he was helped by Bobbili Dor a. The Prime Minister has unveiled a bronze statue of Alluri Sitharama Raju in Andhra Pradesh to celebrate 125th birth anniversary on 4th July 2022.

questions.question: In which year, the Koya rebellion took place in the eastern Godavari tract?
questions.explanation: 1879-1880. The K o y a Re v olts rose under T omma Sor a during 1879-80. Other important revolts are Kol Mutiny (1831), Ho and Munda Uprisings (1820-1837), The Santhal Rebellion (1855-56), Ramosi Uprising (1822- 1829), etc.

questions.question: In which of the following regions did Baba Ramchandra mainly lead the peasant struggle during colonial rule?
questions.explanation: A wadh. He led a peasant movement in Awadh against Talukdars and Landlords.

questions.question: At which of the following places did C. Rajagopalachari break the salt law in the year 1930 ?
questions.explanation: V edar any am. C. Rajagopalachari broke the salt law in 1930 at Vedaranyam, a small coastal town in the then Tanjore District. The ﬁrst Indian governor of free India was C. Rajagopalachari.

questions.question: What was the name of the women's regiment in the Indian National Army founded by Subhash Chandra Bose?
questions.explanation: Rani of Jhansi Regiment.

questions.question: Which maharaja of Manipur signed the Instrument of Accession with the Government of India?
questions.explanation: Bodhchandr a Singh, signed the Instrument of Accession on 11 August 1947, with the assurance of Manipur's internal autonomy. This agreement marked Manipur's entry into the Union of India, though full integration followed with the Manipur Merger Agreement in 1949.

questions.question: Who among the following was one of the founders of the Congress Socialist Party?
questions.explanation: Ram Manohar Lohia. Congress Socialist Party (Socialist caucus within the Indian National Congress) - Founded in 1934. Other founders - Jayaprakash Narayan and Acharya Narendra Dev.

questions.question: In which city was the Jhanda Satyagraha or Flag Satyagraha of 1923 held?
questions.explanation: Nagpur . Flag Satyagraha is a part of civil disobedience during the Indian independence movement.

questions.question: _______was executed at Lahore in November 1915 for his role in the Ghadar Conspiracy in February 1915.
questions.explanation: Kar tar Singh Sar abha. Ghadar Conspiracy (1915) - Plan to initiate a pan-India mutiny in the British Indian Army during World War by the Ghadar Party (United States), the Berlin Committee (Germany), the Indian revolutionary underground in British India and the German Foreign O ﬃ ce.

questions.question: Consider the following statements with regard to Satnami Movement of Central India. a) The movement was founded by Ghasidas. b) The movement was to improve the condition of leather workers. Which of the above statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Both a and b. Guru Ghasidas, a 19th-century advocate of the Satnami sect, founded the Satnami community in Chhattisgarh, promoting Satnam (truth) and equality. His teachings share similarities with Hinduism and Buddhism. The Satnami movement was led by non-Brahman leaders who worked to empower marginalized communities.

questions.question: Who among the following was NOT one of the founders of the Ahrar Movement in British India?
questions.explanation: Maulana Abul Kalam A zad was an Indian independence activist, writer, and a senior leader of the Indian National Congress. questions.explanation: Mother T er esa (from Macedonia) - Nobel Peace Prize (1979), founded the Missionaries of Charity. Saint Bernadette - French saint whose visions led to the founding of the Marian shrine of Lourdes.

questions.question: When did Indigo revolt, a peasant movement, start in India?
questions.explanation: 1859 (March). The Indigo Rebellion (Neel Bidroh) started in Bengal and was a revolt by the farmers against British planters who had forced them to grow indigo under terms that were greatly unfavorable to the farmers.

questions.question: The Paris Indian Society was an Indian nationalist organisation founded in ______.
questions.explanation: 1905 at Paris under the patronage of Madam Bhikaji Cama, Munchershah Burjorji Godrej and S. R. Rana. It was a branch of the Indian Home Rule Society which was established in London in 1905. Bhikaji Cama's important publication is 'Vande Mataram' (1909).

questions.question: In which year was India House established by Shyamji Verma?
questions.explanation: 1905. Other revolutionaries : V.D. Savarkar, Bhikaji Cama, V.N. Chatterjee, Lala Har Dayal, V.V.S. Aiyar, M.P .T. Acharya, P .M. Bapat, etc. Shyamji Krishna Verma founded the Indian Home Rule Society and The Indian Sociologist (Journal) in London. He became the ﬁrst Pr esident of Bombay Arya Samaj.

questions.question: Identify the freedom ﬁghter who, as a child, hated going to school and found it suffocating and oppressive.
questions.explanation: Rabindr anath T agor e. Rabindranath Tagore: Santiniketan (1901), Nobel Prize in Literature (1913, Gitanjali), composed Jana Gana Mana (India’s National Anthem) and Amar Shonar Bangla (Bangladesh’s National Anthem). Other Works: Manasi (1890) [The Ideal One], Sonar Tari (1894) [The Golden Boat], Gitimalya (1914) [Wreath of Songs], and Balaka (1916) [The Flight of Cranes].

questions.question: The claim for ‘swarajya’ or self-government within the British Empire, on the model of self-governing colonies like Australia and Canada, was put forward from the Congress platform in 1905 by:
questions.explanation: Gopal Krishna Gokhale (Diamond of India) was Political Guru of Gandhi, Congress President (1905), established Servants of India Society in 1905, English Newspaper (The Hitavada), founded The Ranade Institute of Economics (1905), ‘Dharmatma Gokhale’ (Writer - Mahatma Gandhi).

questions.question: Who among the following was the founder of the Bhoodan Yojana (Land Gift) movement?
questions.explanation: Achar y a Vinoba Bha v e in 1951, the Bhoodan Movement ( ﬁ rst started in Andhra Pradesh) was initiated at Pochampally village (Telangana). The Bhoodan movement attempted to persuade wealthy landowners to voluntarily give a percentage of their land to landless people.

questions.question: The ﬁrst President of the All India Trade Union Congress was _________.
questions.explanation: Lala Lajpat Rai. All India Trade Union Congress, AITUC (created on October 31, 1920 in Bombay) is the ﬁrst Central Trade Union of India and the second largest trade union federation in India after the Indian National Trade union Congress. In India, Trade Unions in India are registered under the Trade Union Act (1926).

questions.question: Who among the following was the ﬁrst Indian to be selected to the British ‘House of Commons’ on a Liberal Party ticket?
questions.explanation: Dadabhai Naor oji. He was known as India Gladstone. The ﬁrst attempt to calculate National Income of India was made by him. He was the ﬁrst Asian (Indian) member of the British Parliament. He coined the name “Indian National Congress” (INC).

questions.question: Who among the following Indian freedom ﬁghters was fondly called Gandhi Buri (Old Lady Gandhi) for her dedication towards Gandhian principles?
questions.explanation: Matangini Hazr a. Matangini Hazra was an Indian revolutionary and social activist who participated in the Indian independence movement.

questions.question: Avadh Kisan Sabha was set up inthe year ______.
questions.explanation: 1920. Oudh Kisan Sabha (Oudh Farmers Association) was an association formed by Baba Ram Chandra. He was a trade unionist who organized the farmers of Oudh and led theﬁrst protest against the landlords. Q.904 . India and Pakistan had signed the ______, under which the two countries agreed to sort out differences and disputes bilaterally. SSC MTS 11/10/2021 (Morning) (a) Calcutta Pact (b) Shimla Pact (c) Lucknow Pact (d) Agra Pact Sol.904.(b) Shimla P act was signed in 1972 by Zul ﬁ qar Ali Bhutto, the President of Pakistan, and Indira Gandhi, the Prime Minister of India.

questions.question: What was the name of the operation that led to the annexation of the Hyderabad State into India?
questions.explanation: Oper ation P olo - The code name of the Hyderabad "police action" in September 1948. It was a military operation in which the Indian Armed Forces invaded the Nizam-ruled princely state, annexing it into the Indian Union.

questions.question: With whom is the 'Patharughat Uprising' associated?
questions.explanation: P easants’ uprising. It was against the tax policies of the British in 1894. Patharughat is a place in Assam and is currently known as Patharighat.

questions.question: The ______ movement was led by Chaudhary Devi Lal in 1987.
questions.explanation: Ny a y a Y udh. He founded the Lok Dal party.

questions.question: Chandernagore (Chandannagar) was a ______ colony captured by the British Navy on 23 March 1757.
questions.explanation: F r ench. Battle of Chandernagore (1757) -Between French and British (led by Robert Clive) during the Seven Years' War (1756-1763).

questions.question: In which of the following years did Japan invade India resulting in the Battle of Imphal?
questions.explanation: 1944 . The outcome of the battle of Imphal was a turning point of one of the most gruelling campaigns of the Second World War (1939-45).

questions.question: In which of the following years was the Bombay Reorganization Act passed?
questions.explanation: 1960 . After this act, Bombay State was dissolved and split on linguistic lines into the two states of Gujarat (Gujarati speaking population) and Maharashtra (Marathi speaking population).

questions.question: Who among the following is one of the founder members of Bhartiya Jana Sangh?
questions.explanation: Shy ama Pr asad Mukherjee . BJS was an Indian right-wing political party that existed from 1951 to 1977 and was the political arm of Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh (RSS), a Hindu nationalist volunteer organization. Q.912 . A large area of land demarcated as Damin-i-Koh in 1832 was declared as the land of the ______. SSC CHSL 12/04/2021 (Evening) (a) Warlis (b) Paharias (c) Murias (d) Santhals Sol.912.(d) Santhals (Tribal who inhabited the forest of Rajmahal hills). Damin-i-Koh - British promised non-interference in the land.

questions.question: Fort Saint Louis was a ______ fort that stood in Pondicherry on the eastern coast of India.
questions.explanation: F r ench. Fort Saint Louis - Built by Francois Martin and Destroyed by the Britishers in 1761. Q.914 . What was the meaning of ‘Ryot’ in British records in the 18th century? SSC CHSL 19/04/2021 (Evening) (a) Peasants (b) Weavers (c) Traders (d) Jobbers Sol.914.(a) P easants. Ryots were Individual cultivators who had full rights regarding sale, transfer, and leasing of the land as long as they paid the rent under the Ryotwari system (Land revenue paid by the farmers directly to the state). Q.915 . Who was the Nawab of Bhopal that ruled from 1868 to 1901? SSC CHSL 11/08/2021 (Morning) (a) Shah Jahan Begum (b) Begum Sajida Sultan (c) Sikander Jahan Begum (d) Sultan Kaikhusrau Jahan Begum Sol.915.(a) Shah Jahan Begum. Nawab of Bhopal that ruled – Nawab Sikandar Begum:- 1860-1868, Begum Sultan Shah Jehan:-1844-1860 and 1868-1901. Begum Kaikhusrau Jahan:- 1901-1926, Begum Sajida Sultan:- 1961-1995.

questions.question: Who among the following was one of the speakers after Jawaharlal Nehru to address the Parliament on the midnight of 15 August 1947 ?
questions.explanation: Dr . Sar v epalli Radhakrishan (1st Vice President of India) was one of the three main speakers at the Central Hall of Parliament - Other than JL Nehru (Tryst with Destiny Speech) and Chaudhary Khaliquzaman.

questions.question: Who was the chairman of the Economic Programme Committee (EPC) that was formed by the All India Congress Committee in 1947?
questions.explanation: Jawaharlal Nehru . The aim of this committee was to make a plan which could balance private and public partnership and urban and rural economies.

questions.question: In which of the following cities is the tomb of Rani of Jhansi situated?
questions.explanation: Gwalior . The Samadhi of Rani Lakshmi Bai is situated at Phool Bagh. It is to commemorate the great warrior of Jhansi.

questions.question: The princely state of Hyderabad was brought under the Indian Union in the year ______.
questions.explanation: 1948 . After the State Reorganisation Act 1956 Hyderabad was merged with Andhra state and later renamed as Andhra Pradesh.

questions.question: Which leader gave us the C-R Formula?
questions.explanation: Rajagopalachari' s formula (or C. R. formula or Rajaji formula) was a proposal formulated by Chakravarti Rajagopalachari to solve the political deadlock between the All India Muslim League and the Indian National Congress on the independence of British India.

questions.question: ______ was awarded the Nobel Prize in 1913 and a knighthood in 1915.
questions.explanation: Rabindr anath T agor e became the ﬁrst non-European to receive the Nobel Prize for his literary work. He was awarded knighthood in 1915 by King George V.

questions.question: Veteran freedom ﬁghter , social reformer and feminist Savithribai Phule hailed from which of the following states of India?
questions.explanation: Mahar ashtr a. Savithribai Phule - She was the ﬁrst female teacher of India and opened a school for women in 1848, Married to Jyotirao Phule (Founded Satya Shodhak Samaj in 1873 in Pune).

questions.question: Who among the following scholars is associated with the Tibet-home theory of the Aryans?
questions.explanation: Swami Da y anand Sar aswati in his book Satyarth Prakash propounded that the original abode of the Aryans was Tibet. The reasoning behind this was that due to the extreme cold in Tibet, sun and ﬁr e were worshiped and the ﬂor a and fauna described in the Rigveda were found in Tibet.

questions.question: Who among the following was NOT a minister of Cabinet Mission Plan, 1946 ?
questions.explanation: Lor d W a v ell. The members of the Cabinet Mission were Lord Penthick Lawrence, Secretary of State for India, Sir Stafford Cripps, President of the Board of Trade, and A.V Alexander, First Lord of Admiralty.
